one_line_takeaway: In-Context Watermarking embeds detectable, model-agnostic watermarks into LLM output through prompt engineering alone — near-perfect on capable models like gpt-o3-mini and uniquely able to catch dishonest AI-assisted peer reviewers via hidden 'white text' instructions, but its reliability scales with, and is bounded by, the target model's instruction-following ability.
key_finding: ICW works, but its effectiveness is strongly gated by the underlying LLM's instruction-following / in-context-learning capability. On the weaker gpt-4o-mini, the harder schemes largely fail (Initials ROC-AUC 0.572, Acrostics 0.590 in DTS — instructions were essentially ignored), while Unicode still hits 1.000. On the stronger gpt-o3-mini, all four schemes become near-perfect in DTS (Unicode 1.000, Initials 0.999, Lexical 0.995, Acrostics 1.000) and remain strong in the IPI setting (Unicode 1.000, Initials 0.997, Lexical 0.997, Acrostics 0.997 ROC-AUC), matching or beating the post-hoc baselines while uniquely functioning under IPI. Unicode is the most robust to copy-paste/word edits but is fragile to paraphrasing and cross-platform transfer that strips the invisible characters; Initials, Lexical, and Acrostics survive paraphrasing well (paraphrase-attack AUCs 0.887 / 0.924 / 0.922 on gpt-o3-mini). Watermarked text quality stays close to unwatermarked/human (LLM-as-Judge overall: Acrostics 4.813, Lexical 4.808, Unicode 4.810 vs Human 4.235 and unwatermarked 4.992), far above PostMark's 2.997.
core_contribution: The paper introduces In-Context Watermarking (ICW), a framework that embeds detectable watermarks into LLM-generated text purely through prompt engineering, requiring no access to model weights, logits, or the decoding process. Its own contribution is: (1) four concrete ICW strategies at different linguistic granularities — Unicode (insert zero-width space U+200B after each word), Initials (bias words toward a secret 'green' initial-letter set), Lexical (bias toward a secret green-word list, inspired by Kirchenbauer et al.'s green/red list but done via in-context instruction over whole words, not decoding-time logit bias), and Acrostics (constrain sentence-initial letters to spell a secret key sequence); (2) a tailored detection scheme for each (z-statistic tests for Unicode/Initials/Lexical, Levenshtein-distance z-statistic for Acrostics), with a false-alarm-rate guarantee proven in the appendix for Initials and Lexical; and (3) two deployment settings — Direct Text Stamp (DTS), where the watermarking instruction is a system prompt, and Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI), a case study for catching dishonest peer reviewers.
threat_model: The paper's headline threat model is a deliberately REVERSED indirect-prompt-injection scenario, distinct from classic IPI attacks. In the IPI case study there are three entities: paper authors, reviewers, and conference organizers. The benign party (conference organizers) covertly embeds a watermarking instruction into submitted manuscripts using obfuscation such as 'white text' (background-colored) or zero-font-size text. The adversary is a dishonest/lazy reviewer who violates conference policy by feeding the confidential PDF into an LLM to auto-generate a review. When the LLM processes the hidden instruction, its review output carries the detectable watermark, letting organizers identify AI-generated reviews. The detector knows the secret key k and scheme tau; hypothesis testing distinguishes H0 (text generated without knowledge of k,tau) from H1 (generated with it). The paper explicitly notes the reviewer may deploy defenses (detecting/removing the embedded instruction, or prepending 'ignore prior prompts'), but leaves a full attack/defense investigation to future work. In the DTS setting the 'threat' framing is provenance/attribution rather than an adversary — a user or third-party app watermarks outputs for later content attribution.
limitations: The authors themselves flag: (1) heavy dependence on model capability — weak models cannot follow the more complex instructions, so today's reliability rests on frontier models. (2) Lexical ICW struggles with large green-word vocabularies because it stresses long-context retrieval; faithfully simulating decoding-time green/red sampling via in-context token lists remains infeasible. (3) Watermarking instructions are simple; few-shot / chain-of-thought prompting is unexplored. (4) Security is under-examined: Initials is vulnerable to spoofing once the green-letter set is inferred, a fixed Acrostics key yields a conspicuous pattern, and adversarial removal ('ignore prior prompts', detecting/stripping the injected instruction) is only briefly studied in an appendix and otherwise left to future work. Beyond the authors' own list, evaluation is limited to two OpenAI models and English text, and the peer-review deployment raises obvious ethical/consent questions the paper addresses only in an ethics statement.

datasets_used: ELI5 long-form QA dataset (DTS setting; original human answers used as human-text class)
Curated dataset of full ICLR papers 2020-2023 (IPI setting; each complete paper fed as review input)
Canterbury Corpus (used to estimate human initial-letter distribution gamma for Initials ICW detection)
500 watermarked + 500 human texts of ~300 words per evaluation
quantitative_results: DTS ROC-AUC on gpt-o3-mini: Unicode 1.000, Initials 0.999, Lexical 0.995, Acrostics 1.000
DTS ROC-AUC on gpt-4o-mini (weak model): Unicode 1.000, Initials 0.572, Lexical 0.910, Acrostics 0.590
IPI ROC-AUC on gpt-o3-mini: Unicode 1.000, Initials 0.997, Lexical 0.997, Acrostics 0.997
IPI ROC-AUC on gpt-4o-mini: Unicode 0.857, Initials 0.620, Lexical 0.889, Acrostics 0.592
DTS T@1%F on gpt-o3-mini: Unicode 1.000, Initials 0.990, Lexical 0.930, Acrostics 1.000
Paraphrase-attack AUC (DTS, gpt-o3-mini): Initials 0.887, Lexical 0.924, Acrostics 0.922 vs baselines YCZ+23 0.557, PostMark 0.841
Word-replacement-attack AUC (DTS, gpt-o3-mini): Initials 0.999, Acrostics 1.000, Lexical 0.758
LLM-as-Judge overall quality (gpt-o3-mini): Unwatermarked 4.992, Unicode 4.810, Acrostics 4.813, Lexical 4.808, Initials 4.282, Human 4.235, YCZ+23 3.865, PostMark 2.997
baselines_compared: PostMark (Chang et al. 2024) — black-box post-hoc watermarker
YCZ+23 (Yang et al. 2023a) — black-box post-hoc synonym-substitution watermarker
Note: post-hoc baselines are inapplicable in the IPI setting since a dishonest reviewer has no incentive to self-watermark
